html:
Pehr Kalm, born on March 6, 1716, in the parish of Själevad, Ångermanland, and died on November 16, 1779, in Turku (Åbo) in southwestern Finland, was a Swedish botanist, clergyman, and economic writer. He was a professor of economics at the Academy of Turku, but is perhaps best known as one of Carl Linnaeus's students or "apostles."

**Early Life, Education, and Career** Kalm was the son of Gabriel Kalm, a chaplain in Korsnäs, and Catharina Ross (1681–1765), daughter of Herman Williamsson Ross, the mayor of Vasa. In 1750, Kalm married Anna Margareta Sjöman in Philadelphia. She was a widow, having been the wife of Johan Sandin, the pastor of the Swedish congregation in the city, and the daughter of Johan Sjöman, a commissioner at the arsenal in Stockholm. The couple had a son, Pehr Gabriel, who became a major in the Nylands Dragoons regiment.

Kalm's family fled from Finland to what is now Sweden during the Great Northern War. His mother became a widow in Själevad in January 1716, just six weeks before Kalm was born. After the Treaty of Nystad in 1721, Kalm's mother and son moved back to Ostrobothnia, where he attended elementary school in Vasa. In 1735, Kalm enrolled at the University of Turku, where he primarily studied natural sciences. During this time, he became acquainted with Baron Sten Carl Bielke, an assessor at the Turku Court of Appeal who had a strong interest in natural sciences and was a close friend of Carl von Linnaeus.

== Uppsala == Sten Carl Bielke became Kalm's patron, and Bielke decided to finance his continued studies at the University of Uppsala, where Kalm enrolled in 1741. Kalm immediately became a devoted admirer of Carl von Linné, and soon also one of Linné's favorites. He also became a student of Anders Celsius, who introduced him to astronomy and navigation, and how to determine latitude and longitude. From 1740 to 1747, Kalm was in charge of Bielke's experimental garden on his estate, Funbo Lövsta, in the parish of Funbo in Uppland. The estate, located next to Linné's Hammarby, is now the Lövsta research center at SLU (Swedish University of Agricultural Sciences), and an agricultural school. There, among other things, experimental cultivation of legumes was carried out, which were observed to improve soil fertility, although the reason why was not known. Even today, there are still stands of Siberian peashrub (Caragana arborescens) on the Funbo estate, which Kalm and Bielke introduced to Sweden from Siberia in the 1740s. Thanks to Sten Carl Bielke, he also received encouragement to undertake botanical expeditions to Finland, Uppland, and Västmanland from 1740 to 1741. He was then enrolled at Uppsala University and traveled as a student of Linné to Västergötland and Bohuslän, about which he wrote in his "Journey Through West Gothia and Bohus," (published in 1746).

== Member of the Royal Swedish Academy of Sciences, Docent, and Professor in Åbo == Sten Carl Bielke was one of the six founders of the Royal Swedish Academy of Sciences in 1739. Together with Bielke, Kalm traveled to Russia and Little Russia (present-day Ukraine) in 1744, after which he was elected a member of the Royal Swedish Academy of Sciences in 1745, and appointed docent in 1746, and professor of natural history and economics at the Royal Academy in Åbo in 1747, despite not having been promoted. As a professor, he was to teach mineralogy, botany, zoology, and chemistry, as well as conduct research on agriculture, mining, manufacturing, and trade. In the same year, he was also ordained as a priest, but 1747 was also the year he made his longest journey, the trip to America, which would lead to international fame.

== The North American Journey ==

Baron Bielke, in consultation with Linnaeus, proposed to the government and the Royal Swedish Academy of Sciences that a Swedish natural scientist be sent to North America to expand knowledge about the "natural resources" of the new continent and to bring back useful plants. With the joint support of the Royal Swedish Academy of Sciences, the universities of Turku (Åbo) and Uppsala, and the Riksdag (Swedish Parliament), Kalm was authorized to undertake the journey. In the autumn of 1747, he began his journey from Gothenburg to England, where he obtained letters of introduction to Benjamin Franklin, who was then postmaster in Philadelphia. After stops in Norway and London, he arrived in Philadelphia on September 15, 1748, and became good friends with Franklin.

The primary purpose of Kalm's travels was to acquire knowledge about economically valuable plants, including a type of wild raspberry that it was believed could grow in the Finnish marshes, and red mulberry (Morus rubra), which they hoped would be successfully cultivated for silkworm production in Sweden. Both of these species grew far north in America, and it was believed that they could adapt to the Swedish and Finnish climate.

Kalm's reports provide somewhat confusing clues as to which type of mulberry he encountered during his travels. Sometimes they are referred to as black mulberries, sometimes as red mulberries, and some particularly large ones he saw in 1748 near Montreal were called "black American mulberry," Morus nigra americana. The northernmost mulberries he found grew in the area around Albany, New York. Kalm did find the red mulberry and acquired a large number of seeds.

Kalm also described an apple variety, now known as the Rambo apple, which in 1748 grew on Peter Rambo's farm in Raccoon. This is the same Raccoon that gave its name to the main character, John Rambo, in the novel "First Blood," which later inspired the "Rambo" films starring Sylvester Stallone. Peter Rambo stated that...

How certain are we about this?

Information about figures from the Age of Liberty is well-documented through parliamentary records, letters, and archives. However, interpretations of their roles are often influenced by subsequent political affiliations.
tldr: The text describes Pehr Kalm, a Swedish researcher who became famous for his studies of plants and his travels to America in search of useful plants that could benefit Sweden.
lattlast:
Pehr Kalm was a well-educated man who studied natural sciences and economics. He became one of the most important students of the renowned scientist Carl von Linné. During his studies in Uppsala, he learned a great deal about subjects including plants, animals, and how to measure positions on Earth using stars.

Because Kalm was skilled at studying nature, he received support to travel extensively to gather knowledge. He traveled to various parts of Sweden and Finland to study plants, as well as to Russia. His goal was to find plants that could be used to improve agriculture and industry in Sweden.

In 1747, Kalm traveled to North America. The purpose of the trip was to find specific plants, such as cranberries and mulberries, which were hoped to be able to grow in the Swedish climate. He also became friends with the famous American Benjamin Franklin during his time there.

During his travels, Kalm discovered many different types of plants and collected seeds to bring back to Sweden. Among other things, he described a special apple that is now known as the Rambo apple. His work helped to increase knowledge about nature in the New World.
